From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American horror anthology television series (1983–1988)

For the 1990 film based on the series, see [*Tales from the Darkside: The Movie*](/wiki/Tales_from_the_Darkside:_The_Movie "Tales from the Darkside: The Movie"). For the album by Hypetraxx, see [*Tales from the Darkside* (album)](/wiki/Tales_from_the_Darkside_(album) "Tales from the Darkside (album)").

| *Tales from the Darkside* | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Genre | [Horror](/wiki/Horror_fiction "Horror fiction") [Fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy") [Science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") [Comedy drama](/wiki/Comedy_drama "Comedy drama") [Thriller](/wiki/Thriller_(genre) "Thriller (genre)") |
| Created by | [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero") |
| Narrated by | [Paul Sparer](/wiki/Paul_Sparer "Paul Sparer") |
| Theme music composer | [Donald Rubinstein](/wiki/Donald_Rubinstein "Donald Rubinstein") [Erica Lindsay](/wiki/Erica_Lindsay "Erica Lindsay") |
| Country of origin | United States |
| Original language | English |
| No. of seasons | 4 |
| No. of episodes | 92 (2 unaired)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] ([list of episodes](/wiki/List_of_Tales_from_the_Darkside_episodes "List of Tales from the Darkside episodes")) |
| Production | |

| Production companies | [Laurel Entertainment](/wiki/Spelling_Television "Spelling Television") Jaygee Productions [Tribune Entertainment](/wiki/Tribune_Entertainment "Tribune Entertainment") |
| Original release | |
| Network | [Syndicated](/wiki/TV_syndication "TV syndication") |
| Release | October 29, 1983 (1983-10-29) – July 24, 1988 (1988-07-24) |
| Related | |
| *[Monsters](/wiki/Monsters_(American_TV_series) "Monsters (American TV series)")* | |

***Tales from the Darkside*** is an American [horror](/wiki/Horror_film "Horror film") [anthology](/wiki/Anthology_series "Anthology series") television series created by [George A. Romero](/wiki/George_A._Romero "George A. Romero").[[1]](#cite_note-sagar-1) A [pilot](/wiki/Television_pilot "Television pilot") episode was first broadcast on October 29, 1983. The series was picked up for [syndication](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication "Broadcast syndication"), and the first season premiered on September 30, 1984. The show would run for a total of four seasons, until its cancelation on July 24, 1988.

The series spans various genres of [speculative fiction](/wiki/Speculative_fiction "Speculative fiction"), including [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction") and [fantasy](/wiki/Fantasy "Fantasy") in addition to horror, and many episodes have [darkly comic](/wiki/Black_comedy "Black comedy") elements.

## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_the_Darkside&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Production")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Tales_from_the_Darkside "Special:EditPage/Tales from the Darkside") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(June 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

The movie *[Creepshow](/wiki/Creepshow "Creepshow")* was released in 1982. It was based on the [EC](/wiki/EC_Comics "EC Comics") [horror comic books](/wiki/Horror_comics "Horror comics") of the [1950s](/wiki/1950s_in_comics "1950s in comics"), such as *[Tales from the Crypt](/wiki/Tales_from_the_Crypt_(comics) "Tales from the Crypt (comics)")* and *[The Vault of Horror](/wiki/The_Vault_of_Horror_(comics) "The Vault of Horror (comics)")*. In light of the film's moderate success, its producer, [Laurel Entertainment](/wiki/Richard_P._Rubinstein#Laurel_Entertainment "Richard P. Rubinstein"), decided to explore the potential for a television series based on the feature. However, [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros.") had the rights to some elements of *Creepshow*. Laurel chose to move forward with a series that omitted aspects of the movie that were owned by Warner Bros. The result was *Tales from the Darkside*, which explored the same themes as *Creepshow*, but discarded the framing device and other elements that were based directly on the comics.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Some episodes were adapted from the works of well-known authors, or written by the authors themselves. Stories or teleplays by [Frederik Pohl](/wiki/Frederik_Pohl "Frederik Pohl"), [Harlan Ellison](/wiki/Harlan_Ellison "Harlan Ellison"),[[1]](#cite_note-sagar-1) [Clive Barker](/wiki/Clive_Barker "Clive Barker"),[[1]](#cite_note-sagar-1) [Michael Bishop](/wiki/Michael_Bishop_(author) "Michael Bishop (author)"), [Robert Bloch](/wiki/Robert_Bloch "Robert Bloch"), [John Cheever](/wiki/John_Cheever "John Cheever"), [Michael McDowell](/wiki/Michael_McDowell_(author) "Michael McDowell (author)"), and [Fredric Brown](/wiki/Fredric_Brown "Fredric Brown") were used over the course of the series. Two episodes, "[Word Processor of the Gods](/wiki/Word_Processor_of_the_Gods "Word Processor of the Gods")"[[3]](#cite_note-3) and "[Sorry, Right Number](/wiki/Sorry,_Right_Number "Sorry, Right Number")",[[4]](#cite_note-4) were based on short stories by [Stephen King](/wiki/Stephen_King "Stephen King"). The latter was adapted for television by King himself.

The show had a low budget, and often utilized only a single set or location throughout entire episodes, similar to a [stage play](/wiki/Play_(theater) "Play (theater)").[[5]](#cite_note-news-5) It was a bi-coastal production, with some episodes being shot in [New York](/wiki/New_York_(State) "New York (State)") and others in [California](/wiki/California "California").[[5]](#cite_note-news-5)

### Opening and closing sequences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_the_Darkside&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Opening and closing sequences")]

In the vein of previous anthology series *[The Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone_(1959_TV_series) "The Twilight Zone (1959 TV series)")* and *[The Outer Limits](/wiki/The_Outer_Limits_(1963_TV_series) "The Outer Limits (1963 TV series)")*, each episode opens with a [montage](/wiki/Montage_(filmmaking) "Montage (filmmaking)") and an unseen narrator. Several [bucolic](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/bucolic "wikt:bucolic") scenes are shown, followed by a slow pan through a forest filled with barren trees, which in turn yields to a fence-lined path through a meadow. This last shot fades into a black-and-white [negative image](/wiki/Negative_image "Negative image") as the series title appears.

> Man lives in the sunlit world of what he *believes* to be reality. But ... there is, unseen by most, an underworld ... a place that is just as real, but not as brightly lit. A *darkside*.

The closing credits are displayed against the final, negative image of the opening.

> The darkside is always there, waiting for us to enter, waiting to enter us. Until next time, *try* to enjoy the daylight.

The narration is performed by [Paul Sparer](/wiki/Paul_Sparer "Paul Sparer"), and was written by Romero. The opening and closing themes are performed by [Donald Rubinstein](/wiki/Donald_Rubinstein "Donald Rubinstein"), who co-wrote the music with [Erica Lindsay](/wiki/Erica_Lindsay "Erica Lindsay").

The entire opening sequence of the show was filmed outside of Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, not far from where George Romero filmed most of his movies in the 70s and early 80s. Romero was familiar with the area because he had lived there for many years and scouted numerous filming locations when he went into producing the Tales From the Darkside TV series. The bridge location used to be called [Deihls Bridge](/wiki/Diehls_Covered_Bridge "Diehls Covered Bridge"), but is now known as Turner's Bridge. Some of the forest footage and the creek flowing over the rocks was filmed in Clear Creek State Forest just north of Pittsburgh. Other filming locations such as the farm, the fields, and the road with the old wooden fence were filmed in or around Boswell, PA and Ligonier, PA. However, much of that area has changed dramatically since the opening was shot in 1983.

## Episodes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_the_Darkside&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Episodes")]

Main article: [List of *Tales from the Darkside* episodes](/wiki/List_of_Tales_from_the_Darkside_episodes "List of Tales from the Darkside episodes")

| Season | | Episodes | Originally aired | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| First aired | Last aired |
|  | Pilot | 1 | October 29, 1983 | October 29, 1983 |
|  | [1](/wiki/List_of_Tales_from_the_Darkside_episodes#Season_1_(1984–85) "List of Tales from the Darkside episodes") | 23 | September 30, 1984 | August 4, 1985 |
|  | [2](/wiki/List_of_Tales_from_the_Darkside_episodes#Season_2_(1985–86) "List of Tales from the Darkside episodes") | 24 | September 29, 1985 | July 13, 1986 |
|  | [3](/wiki/List_of_Tales_from_the_Darkside_episodes#Season_3_(1986–87) "List of Tales from the Darkside episodes") | 22 | September 28, 1986 | May 17, 1987 |
|  | [4](/wiki/List_of_Tales_from_the_Darkside_episodes#Season_4_(1987–88) "List of Tales from the Darkside episodes") | 20 | September 27, 1987 | July 24, 1988 |

## Broadcast and rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_the_Darkside&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Broadcast and rights")]

In its initial run, [LBS Communications](/wiki/LBS_Communications "LBS Communications") distributed *Tales from the Darkside* in [first-run syndication](/wiki/First-run_syndication "First-run syndication") through the [barter deal](/wiki/Broadcast_syndication#Types_of_deals "Broadcast syndication") method. It aired on both independent stations and local network affiliates, typically being shown in late night timeslots.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Despite being a low budget syndicated show with little promotion, it managed to outperform higher profile network anthology shows from this period. These include [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s *[Amazing Stories](/wiki/Amazing_Stories_(1985_TV_series) "Amazing Stories (1985 TV series)")* and the 1985 *[Twilight Zone](/wiki/The_Twilight_Zone_(1985_TV_series) "The Twilight Zone (1985 TV series)")* revival, which both debuted on major networks in primetime while *Tales from the Darkside* was airing in syndication.[[5]](#cite_note-news-5)

Distribution rights later passed to [Worldvision Enterprises](/wiki/Worldvision_Enterprises "Worldvision Enterprises") after [Aaron Spelling Productions](/wiki/Aaron_Spelling_Productions "Aaron Spelling Productions") acquired Worldvision Enterprises and Laurel Productions in 1989. The rights are currently held by [CBS Media Ventures](/wiki/CBS_Media_Ventures "CBS Media Ventures"), a subdivision of [Paramount Global](/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global"), due to CBS's absorption of Aaron Spelling Productions in the latter part of the 1990s. Paramount had handled the theatrical distribution of the 1990 *[Tales from the Darkside](/wiki/Tales_from_the_Darkside:_The_Movie "Tales from the Darkside: The Movie")* movie (which was one of Laurel Entertainment's last productions), although this was still several years before they inherited the rights to the show through CBS's absorption of Aaron Spelling Productions.

## Home video

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_the_Darkside&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Home video")]

The series was released on [VHS](/wiki/VHS "VHS") with the episodes' original music. Later DVD releases altered or omitted some of this music.

[Paramount Home Entertainment](/wiki/Paramount_Home_Entertainment "Paramount Home Entertainment"), through [CBS Home Entertainment](/wiki/CBS_Home_Entertainment "CBS Home Entertainment") (sister company to [Spelling Television](/wiki/Spelling_Television "Spelling Television"), the successor to Laurel), released the series on DVD, beginning on February 10, 2009. The company rereleased the complete series on [Region 1](/wiki/DVD_region_code#Region_codes_and_countries "DVD region code") DVD in 2018. British company [Revelation Films](/wiki/Revelation_Films "Revelation Films") sublicensed it from CBS/Paramount, and released the entire series on DVD in Region 2 in 2013. In [Region 4](/wiki/DVD_region_code#Region_codes_and_countries "DVD region code") (Australia) the show was released on DVD by CBS/Paramount, in association with local distributor [Shock](/wiki/Shock_Records "Shock Records"). The series has never been released on Blu-Ray, likely due to the poor quality of the footage from the original master tapes.

| Title | Episodes | Release dates | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Region 1 | Region 2 |
| The First Season | 24 | February 10, 2009 | November 21, 2011 |
| The Second Season | 24 | October 27, 2009 | February 20, 2012 |
| The Third Season | 22 | April 27, 2010 | May 7, 2012 |
| The Final Season | 22 (2 unaired) | October 19, 2010 | August 6, 2012 |
| The Complete Series | 92 (2 unaired) | October 19, 2010 | October 28, 2013 |

An audio commentary by Executive Producer George A. Romero on the pilot episode, "Trick or Treat", as well as two unaired eplsodes, "Akhbar's Daughter" and "Attic Suite", are cited on the cover of the DVD.

## Awards and nominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_the_Darkside&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Awards and nominations")]

## Subsequent series and movie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_the_Darkside&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Subsequent series and movie")]

*Tales from the Darkside* executive producer [Richard P. Rubinstein](/wiki/Richard_P._Rubinstein "Richard P. Rubinstein") and his company Laurel would go on to make the horror anthology series *[Monsters](/wiki/Monsters_(American_TV_series) "Monsters (American TV series)")*, which premiered in 1988 and ran for three seasons,[[7]](#cite_note-7) as well as *[Tales from the Darkside: The Movie](/wiki/Tales_from_the_Darkside:_The_Movie "Tales from the Darkside: The Movie")*, which was released theatrically in 1990.[[8]](#cite_note-8) A sequel to the film was announced, but never made.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In November 2013, it was reported that [Joe Hill](/wiki/Joe_Hill_(writer) "Joe Hill (writer)"), [Alex Kurtzman](/wiki/Alex_Kurtzman "Alex Kurtzman"), and [Roberto Orci](/wiki/Roberto_Orci "Roberto Orci") were developing a reboot of the series for [The CW](/wiki/The_CW "The CW"), with [CBS Television Studios](/wiki/CBS_Television_Studios "CBS Television Studios").[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 2014, Hill said that he would serve as creative director, and guide the show. Added Hill, "*Darkside* is a loose reboot ... It tells stories about different characters. It also tells an ongoing story. I love the original *Tales From The Darkside*, *The Outer Limits*, and *The Twilight Zone*, but I think in a post-*[X-Files](/wiki/The_X-Files "The X-Files")* world there's really no room for a straight anthology show. There has to be more. I like stories that work like puzzle boxes, every episode is turning another facet. We have something a little like that in *Darkside* ... every episode is a different story but three or four episodes in, you're going, 'Wait a minute, these parts actually all go together, don't they?'"

In February 2014, The CW gave the reboot a pilot order. Shooting began on March 19 and wrapped on April 4. The CW did not pick up the series, however. It then was offered to other networks unsuccessfully.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## In other media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tales_from_the_Darkside&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: In other media")]

The book *Tales from the Darkside: Volume One*, published in 1988, consisted of stories and episode novelizations.

Scripts written by Hill for the proposed reboot were adapted into a four-issue comic book series published in June 2016, followed by a collection of scripts in book form in October 2016. Both the comics and the book were released by [IDW Publishing](/wiki/IDW_Publishing "IDW Publishing").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15)
